nativeRange.habitat: The Blue Tip Longfin Grouper inhabits inshore coral and rocky reefs, typically at depths of 10–20 m. It is closely associated with caves, crevices, and sheltered overhangs, where it shelters and forages near the seafloor.

diet.notes: Has been documented feeding on meaty foods such as krill, shrimp, and small fish. In captivity, generally takes frozen meaty preparations; live saltwater feeder shrimp or similar are sometimes used to entice newly introduced specimens. Will consume small fish or crustaceans that fit in its mouth.

goodWith: robust fish too large to be swallowed
avoidWith: small fish that fit in the mouth

summary: The Blue Tip Longfin Grouper (Paraplesiops poweri), also known as the Northern Blue Devil, is a rare Australian roundhead notable for its elongated grayish body and strikingly blue-edged fins. Best suited to intermediate keepers with a cave-rich reef system.
careNotes:
The Blue Tip Longfin Grouper belongs to the family Plesiopidae — a group of secretive, cave-associated marine fish sometimes called roundheads — and is found in the tropical and subtropical coastal waters of northern Australia. Despite being sold under the 'grouper' trade name, this species is not a true grouper (family Serranidae) but is instead a roundhead, and is closely related to the Western Blue Devil (Paraplesiops meleagris) of more southerly Australian waters. It is generally considered a rare find in the aquarium trade.

The species has an elongated, grayish body with large fins outlined in vivid blue — the distinctive colouration that gives rise to its trade names. Adults are recorded reaching up to 19 cm, though some sources suggest a smaller maximum standard length, likely reflecting differences in how length is measured. The related P. meleagris reaches approximately 33 cm, so 19 cm appears a plausible upper bound for this smaller tropical species.

As a cave-dwelling, benthic species, the Blue Tip Longfin Grouper tends to shelter among rockwork, crevices, and overhangs, and may be cryptic and less active during daylight hours. A tank with ample live rock and well-formed hiding spaces is generally advised, with a minimum tank size of around 300 litres. Water temperature is best kept in the range of 19–25°C, which reflects this species' preference for somewhat warmer conditions than the more temperate P. meleagris, consistent with its more northerly distribution.

In terms of temperament, the Blue Tip Longfin Grouper tends to be peaceful toward fish too large to swallow but is an opportunistic predator and will consume small fish or crustaceans that fit in its mouth. For this reason it is best kept with tankmates of similar or larger size and generally should not be housed with ornamental shrimp or small invertebrates. It is broadly considered reef-safe with caution — it will not typically nip at corals, but will actively hunt mobile invertebrates and small fishes within the system.
breedingNotes: Males exhibit paternal care, guarding eggs after spawning. Captive breeding has not been widely documented in the hobby literature and is generally considered difficult, in part because of how rarely this species appears in the trade.

primary.name: LiveAquaria
primary.notes: Primary care sheet. Recommends 70 gal minimum (~265 L), max size 7.5 inches (~19 cm), pH 8.1–8.4, SG 1.020–1.025, dKH 8–12, temperature 65–75°F (18.3–23.9°C), care level moderate, reef compatibility 'with caution', diet carnivore on meaty foods. Species listed as rare in the trade.

Reef2Reef secondary (403) and BlueZoo Aquatics (product not found) both failed; FishBase used as primary secondary.

fish.breedingNotes: Males guard eggs after spawning. Captive breeding is rarely documented and is generally considered difficult, reflecting the rarity of this species in the trade.
